title: Home Health Care Services

labor_share.method: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 621610 ($71,424,773,339) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 621610 ($113,350,417,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

occupations.title: Home Health and Personal Care Aides
Licensed Practical and Licensed Vocational Nurses

n_band.derivation: margin 0.1580 (margins.json[62]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Hospitals/Healthcare Facilities EBITDA/Sales 15.80% (closest named bucket for provider economics; public support-services bucket at 3.87% reflects a different business model)); revenue/employee $72,294 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 621610); class <5: 11,187 firms, avg revenue/firm $242,470 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $38,310 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 3,386 firms, avg revenue/firm $660,235 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $104,317 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 3,828 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,118,957 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $176,795 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 7,049 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,771,668 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $437,923 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 1,983 firms, avg revenue/firm $10,545,427 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,666,177 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 765 firms, avg revenue/firm $83,228,652 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $13,150,127 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 1,983
n_band.margin_source: Damodaran Jan 2026: Hospitals/Healthcare Facilities EBITDA/Sales 15.80% (closest named bucket for provider economics; public support-services bucket at 3.87% reflects a different business model)

breakdown_by_role.role: Home Health and Personal Care Aides (31-1120)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: In-home physical care (bathing, transfers, meal prep, companionship) is the product; mature AI does not replace the visit. Tiny sliver for EVV/app-based visit documentation and check-in overhead only.

Skilled visits are physical and persist, but a large slice of RN time is OASIS assessments, care-plan paperwork, and coding-adjacent documentation. Vendor deployments report 40-55% documentation time savings (AutomationEdge/industry deployments); documentation is roughly a third of RN episode time, so AI reclaims ~30% of the role's labor cost.
Hands-on clinical support in the home; lighter documentation burden than RNs (no OASIS ownership). Small documentation sliver only.
Physical hands-on care like aides; visit-note sliver only.

Physical home visits persist; documentation/plan-of-care paperwork sliver is real but smaller than RN OASIS burden. PDGM already removed therapy-volume incentives in 2020 - the visit decline is a payment artifact, not AI substitution, and is not counted here.
QA record review, scheduling oversight, utilization reporting automate substantially; clinical escalation, survey readiness, and family communication persist.
Relationship and assessment work persists; resource-lookup and documentation slivers automate.
Intake/referral processing, eligibility checks, filing, claims-status work - the clean AI surface; some phone/exception handling persists.
Scheduling, records requests, referral paperwork - same clean surface as clerks.
Same logic as PT: physical visit persists, documentation sliver automates.

breakdown_by_role.role: Remainder / unlisted tail (schedulers, coders, billers, intake, recruiters, HR, liaisons)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Per prompt guidance the tail contains ~5-8pp of pure office/scheduling/billing/coding (automatable at clerk-like ~0.6) plus recruiting (partial: screening/outreach automate, interviews persist) and hospital-liaison marketing (relationship work, persists). Blended 0.35.

pi_dist.rationale: Payers, not clients, control pricing, and the clawback mechanism is documented and recurring. Medicare FFS PDGM episodic payment lets an efficient agency keep gains only between rate cycles: CMS finalized permanent and temporary behavioral-adjustment cuts in the CY2026 HH PPS final rule, and MedPAC (March 2026) projects a 19% FFS margin for 2026 and recommends a 7% base-rate cut for CY2027 - measured efficiency is transferred back to the payer on a multi-year lag. Medicaid HCBS hourly rates are state-set, with wage pass-through laws (e.g., NY wage parity) converting labor-cost savings into mandated wages. MA plans negotiate per-visit rates downward and reprice networks. Private-pay hourly is client-paid but competitively bid in a low-entry-barrier market. Referral flow is owned by hospital discharge planners and MA/ACO networks, not the agency. Some retention (0.35 not 0.15) because rebasing lags run 2-4 years and private-pay/MA-mix agencies keep more.

pi_moat.rationale: The product is physically delivered in the home, so clients cannot bypass the agency with generic AI at all - that bypass threat is essentially zero. Medicare-certified home health carries certification plus CON/licensure moratoria in some states (real moat). But the dominant band-size sub-segment is weighted toward Medicaid HCBS/personal care and private-duty, where entry barriers are licensure-only in many states, caregiver turnover runs 60-80%, and census durability hinges on individual liaison relationships and payer network contracts that can reprice or churn post-acquisition. Blend: high bypass protection, moderate-to-low entry/switching protection.

t50_years.derivation: current_adoption_pct ~24% materially invested in AI (Axxess/AxisCare 2026 survey data: 'fewer than one in four organizations have made any AI-specific investment'). Historical analog: EHR adoption in home health agencies went from ~41% (2007 NCHS) to over 75% (2017 ONC), crossing 50% around 2010 - roughly 12+ years from wide availability to majority adoption. AI tools ride the now-installed EHR/agency-management platforms (Axxess, WellSky, AxisCare embed AI directly), stated intent is very high (91% using-or-planning per 2026 industry survey of 400+ leaders), and the tools attack the #1 operator pains (documentation time, scheduling churn) - so diffusion should run roughly 3x the EHR pace. From 24% to 50% at that pace: ~3.5 years.

current_adoption_pct.source: myEZCare 2026 Home Care Industry Report (aggregating Axxess 2026 Industry Growth Insights Report and AxisCare 2026 Home Care Industry Survey)
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: 'fewer than one in four organizations have made any AI-specific investment' (~24%); 64% of leaders cite scheduling/shift matching as AI's most impactful application; corroborating: HHCN 2025 research found only 19% of home care providers consider themselves AI early adopters, 46% 'selective'. Material = paid deployment of AI in documentation, scheduling, coding, or intake (not pilots or intent).

historical_analogs.value: EHR adoption in home health agencies: ~41% (2007, NCHS National Home and Hospice Care Survey) to over 75% (2017, ONC data brief) - crossed ~50% circa 2010, i.e. roughly 12-15 years from wide availability of point-of-care systems to majority adoption; telehealth in HHAs stayed low for over a decade despite availability.
historical_analogs.source: ONC Data Brief: Electronic Health Record Adoption and Interoperability among U.S. Skilled Nursing Facilities and Home Health Agencies in 2017
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: In 2017, over three-quarters of HHAs reported they had adopted EHRs; a decade earlier surveys showed minimal uptake of EHR and telehealth use (PMC10752454 multi-methods study).

owners_60plus_pct.source: InCorp / national small-business owner age statistics (no home-care-specific owner-age dataset located; NAHC/Alliance and Activated Insights benchmarks not accessible for owner age)
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: '52.3 percent of US business owners are 55 or older, with 29.5 percent in the 55 to 64 cohort and 22.8 percent age 65 plus' - interpolating, 60+ is approximately 22.8% + ~half of the 55-64 cohort = ~37.5%. Home care skews slightly younger (franchise entry wave post-2010) but band-size (100-499 employee) firms skew toward older established founders; used the national figure.

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: A generic boomer succession wave is documented economy-wide (e.g., '58% of these owners have no documented transition plan'), but no home-health-specific succession-shortage documentation was found from NAHC/the Alliance or Activated Insights; heavy PE buyer presence suggests exit demand is met. Flag left false.

active_consolidators.source: CT Acquisitions Home Health PE Roll-Up Tracker 2026
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: 'Home Health PE Roll-Up Tracker 2026: 32 Active Platforms' - tracker lists PE-backed platforms with add-ons in 2024-2026 including Choice Health at Home/Trive ('twenty-plus deals since the 2020 Trive entry', latest Feb 2026), BrightSpring (107 Amedisys branches, Dec 2025), Bristol Hospice/Webster, Care Advantage/Searchlight. Corroborated by Mertz Taggart: 105 home care deals closed in 2025, 21 more than 2024.

buy_mult.source: CT Acquisitions Home Health PE Roll-Up Tracker 2026 (corroborated by Mertz Taggart commentary and FOCUS)
buy_mult.figure_quoted: 'Small HHA ($200K-$1M EBITDA): 4x-6x' - midpoint 5.0x adjusted EBITDA for single-location/small agencies; non-medical/private-duty personal care clears 3-5x, Medicare-certified regional agencies 6-9x. Multiples quoted on adjusted (owner-comp-normalized) EBITDA per broker convention.

exit_mult.source: CT Acquisitions tracker / Enhabit take-private (corroborated by FOCUS home health & hospice multiples dashboard)
exit_mult.figure_quoted: 'Enhabit's Kinderhook take-private closed at ~10.2x EBITDA on ~$108 million EBITDA'; platform-tier HHA ($20M+ EBITDA) range quoted at 10x-14x. Used the actual closed transaction at the conservative end of the platform range.

pricing_structure.value: Per-unit administered/negotiated pricing, no client-set fees: Medicare FFS pays PDGM 30-day episodic prospective payments (largest single payer, ~$52B in 2023); Medicaid HCBS pays state-set hourly rates (some with statutory wage pass-through); Medicare Advantage pays negotiated per-visit rates (share growing with MA penetration); private-pay/LTC insurance pays hourly fees. Band-size agencies are typically Medicaid/MA/private-duty-weighted mixes; virtually no revenue is billed on cost-plus or open-book terms, but rates are payer-administered rather than operator-set.
pricing_structure.source: MedPAC March 2026 Report to the Congress, Ch. 8 (home health) and CMS CY2026 HH PPS final rule
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: MedPAC projects a 19% FFS Medicare margin for freestanding HHAs in 2026 (21.2% in 2024) and recommends Congress reduce the home health base payment rate by 7% for CY2027; CMS finalized permanent and temporary PDGM behavioral adjustments to the CY2026 base rate.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: V_raw = 0.1115 implies a gross EBITDA uplift of ~11.1pp of revenue at full realization (realistically 3-4.5pp near-term after partial realization and before payer clawback). A = 2.857 < 5, so the inconsistency trigger (uplift < 3pp with A >= 5) does not apply; the pair is consistent - meaningful but wrapper-limited value, moderate adoption speed.

terminal_value.justification: The product is a human caregiver in the home - aides, nurses and therapists are ~75% of the wage bill and mature AI does not replace the visit; paid demand is demographically expanding. AI cuts the operator's admin/documentation costs, it does not replace the operator's product. But durability of the OPERATOR'S AI cost advantage differs by payer: Medicare-certified skilled care - the advantage is temporary; CMS PDGM behavioral adjustments (CY2026 final rule) and MedPAC's recommended 7% cut on 19% projected margins are a documented recurring mechanism transferring measured efficiency to the payer within 1-2 rate cycles. Medicaid personal care - state-set hourly rates with wage pass-through strings convert labor-cost savings into mandated wages; the operator keeps little. Private-pay - the operator keeps gains longest, but licensure-only entry means competition erodes the spread. Demand is DURABLE; the AI margin spread is a wasting asset, which is priced in C (1.925), not in the terminal-value class.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: Confirmed: V was built solely from the OEWS role mix and within-role task analysis (documentation time, scheduling/intake/billing surfaces); A was derived from the Axxess/AxisCare adoption surveys and the EHR diffusion analog. Neither references deal counts, consolidation waves, nor PE activity. Optum/Amedisys, BrightSpring, Choice, Addus and the Mertz Taggart deal data were used only for B (active_consolidators) and M (multiples).
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Billing mix is entirely per-unit administered/negotiated rates (Medicare PDGM episodes, Medicaid hourly, MA per-visit, private-pay hourly) - no cost-plus or open-book contracts, so there is no automatic contractual pass-through. Instead the deflation mechanism is periodic payer rate-setting: PDGM rebasing/behavioral adjustments and MedPAC-recommended cuts claw back measured efficiency on a lag, Medicaid wage pass-through laws convert savings to wages, and MA networks reprice. This is reflected in pi_dist = 0.35 (operator keeps gains only between rate cycles and on the private-pay slice); pi_moat = 0.55 reflects zero client-bypass risk (physical product) offset by low entry barriers in the dominant personal-care sub-segment.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: A ranges 1.667-4.0; S ranges ~3.30-3.93 (S scales as (A/2.857)^0.2 around 3.673)

top_uncertain_inputs.plausible_range: 4.0-6.5 (3-5x private-duty vs 6-9x Medicare-certified; band mix uncertain)

reviewer_note: HETEROGENEITY (first-order, per prompt trap list): 621610 blends Medicare-certified skilled home health, Medicaid HCBS/personal care, and private-duty non-medical home care. The 1,983 band firms (all in the 100-499 employee class, ~$10.5M avg revenue, $72K revenue/employee) are consistent with aide-heavy staffing; I judged the dominant band sub-segment to be Medicaid-HCBS/personal-care-weighted agencies (often with mixed skilled lines) and scored on that basis - hence pi_moat 0.55 (licensure-only entry in many states) rather than the higher moat a pure Medicare-certified/CON-state segment would earn, and buy_mult 5.0x blending the 3-5x private-duty and 6-9x certified ranges. heterogeneous = true. DATASET CONFLICTS: none material; the 15.8% Damodaran hospital-bucket margin used in the n_band derivation is above MedPAC's all-payer reality for personal-care-heavy agencies (Medicare FFS margins ~19% but Medicaid/private-duty margins are single-digit-to-low-teens), which may overstate band EBITDA and thus overstate n_band slightly - used as given per rule 6. OWNERS INPUT is the weakest link: national owner-age interpolation, ESTIMATE quality; succession bonus withheld for lack of industry-specific documentation. FORMULA VS INTUITION: the formula S=3.67 feels right directionally, but note M=4.16 rides on a genuinely wide small-agency multiple spread and C=1.925 correctly encodes that payer clawback - not AI capability - is the binding constraint on operator value here. MA-penetration margin squeeze was kept out of the AI-effect estimates per the trap list. Labor share 0.6718 is mostly the product (care hours), not overhead - the automatable share of 0.166 reflects the admin/documentation wrapper only.
